target.name: Agility Robotics
target.founder: Jonathan Hurst, Damion Shelton, Mikhail Jones

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series D → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.5x) to $5.2B total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Robotics & Manufacturing. Range: $1.0B - $2.3B.

private.name: Exotec
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series D), post-money $2B

private.name: Gecko Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series D)

data.narrative:
Agility Robotics, a Series D robotics and manufacturing company, has raised $400M+ to date. The valuation analysis draws on two methodologies applied against a peer set of nine comparable companies—one public and eight private—including Exotec, Divergent, Locus Robotics, Bedrock Robotics, and Dexterity at similar or adjacent stages.

The stage-progression method, anchoring on the $5.2B implied by total capital raised and applying a 1.5x step-up multiplier typical for Series D advancement, yields $7.8B. The peer-median approach across four same-sector comparables (Divergent at $2.3B Series E, Exotec at $2.0B Series D, Hadrian at $1.6B Series C, and Nimble Robotics at $1.0B Series C) produces a $1.8B implied valuation, with a range of $1.0B to $2.3B.

Triangulating these two methods generates a median valuation of $4.8B, with a low of $1.5B and a high of $9.0B. The wide dispersion reflects material tension between the stage-progression approach—which extrapolates from total capital as a proxy for value—and peer-median benchmarking, which anchors to realized transactions. Key uncertainties include whether the $5.2B raised proxy accurately reflects intrinsic value versus market exuberance, and whether Agility's operational maturity and market position justify a premium or discount relative to the peer median.
